result.markdown:
---
title: "Khan SRBCT cancer classification"
subtitle: "$p = 2308$ genes, 4 cancer types, $n = 83$ â€” and the answer is twelve genes"
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
R code is executed; outputs below are real. Click the **Python** tab for the equivalent script.
:::

## The data

Khan et al. (2001) profiled gene expression for small round blue-cell tumours, a family of four childhood cancers that look nearly identical under a microscope:

- **BL** â€” Burkitt lymphoma
- **EWS** â€” Ewing's sarcoma
- **NB** â€” neuroblastoma
- **RMS** â€” rhabdomyosarcoma

Each tumour is described by **2308 cDNA microarray probes**. The training set has 63 samples; the test set has 20. The classical version of the question is: *can you train a classifier that distinguishes the four tumour types using only a handful of genes?*

The CSVs in `data/` are formatted with the class label in the first column (`y`) and the 2308 probes in columns `X1`â€“`X2308`.

## Load the data

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
train <- read.csv("../data/Khan_train.csv")
test  <- read.csv("../data/Khan_test.csv")

x_train <- as.matrix(train[, -1]); y_train <- train$y
x_test  <- as.matrix(test[, -1]);  y_test  <- test$y

dim(x_train); table(y_train)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1]   63 2308
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
y_train
 1  2  3  4 
 8 23 12 20 
```


:::
:::


## Python

```python
import pandas as pd

train = pd.read_csv("../data/Khan_train.csv")
test  = pd.read_csv("../data/Khan_test.csv")

X_train = train.drop(columns="y").to_numpy()
y_train = train["y"].to_numpy()
X_test  = test.drop(columns="y").to_numpy()
y_test  = test["y"].to_numpy()

print(X_train.shape)
print(pd.Series(y_train).value_counts())
```

:::

## Fit COMBSS

Run COMBSS for $k = 1, \ldots, 20$ with multinomial logistic loss and the test set as validation:

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(combss)
fit_khan <- combss(x_train, y_train,
                   x_val = x_test, y_val = y_test,
                   family = "multinomial", q = 20)
fit_khan
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
COMBSS fit
  family:    multinomial
  n, p:      63, 2308
  q:         20
  lam_ridge: 0
  best k:    12
  accuracy:  1
  subset:    187, 246, 509, 545, 910, 1074, 1319, 1389, 1645, 1954, 1955, 2050
```


:::
:::


## Python

```python
from combss import combss

fit_khan = combss(X_train, y_train,
                  X_val=X_test, y_val=y_test,
                  family="multinomial", q=20)
print(fit_khan)
```

:::

## How many genes does COMBSS pick?

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fit_khan$best_k
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 12
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
fit_khan$subset_list[[ fit_khan$best_k ]]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1]  187  246  509  545  910 1074 1319 1389 1645 1954 1955 2050
```


:::
:::


## Python

```python
print(fit_khan.best_k)
print(fit_khan.subset_list[fit_khan.best_k - 1])
```

:::

## Test-set accuracy at each $k$

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## R


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(seq_along(fit_khan$accuracy_path), fit_khan$accuracy_path, type = "b", pch = 19,
     xlab = "k (number of selected genes)", ylab = "Test accuracy")
abline(v = fit_khan$best_k, lty = 2)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Accuracy as a function of the number of selected genes.](03-khan_files/figure-html/khan-acc-r-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Python

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.plot(range(1, len(fit_khan.accuracy_path) + 1), fit_khan.accuracy_path, "o-")
plt.axvline(fit_khan.best_k, ls="--")
plt.xlabel("k (number of selected genes)")
plt.ylabel("Test accuracy")
plt.show()
```

:::

## The headline

Twelve genes are enough to separate the four tumour types â€” perfect classification on the held-out 20 samples, drawn from 2308 candidates. The R and Python implementations agree on the same twelve genes.

A lasso baseline on the same training data selects an order of magnitude more genes for comparable accuracy (see [Comparisons](05-comparisons.qmd)).

## Reference

Khan, J. et al. (2001). *Classification and diagnostic prediction of cancers using gene expression profiling and artificial neural networks*. **Nature Medicine** 7, 673â€“679.
